speaker: interviewer
text: The date is July 16, 1996, survivor is Benjamin Jacobs, last name at birth, Jakubowicz. The interviewer is Louise Bobrow. The city is Brookline in the state of Massachusetts. The country is United States of America. And the language is in English. My name is Louise Bobrow. Today's date is July 16, 1996. I am conducting an interview with survivor Benjamin Jacobs. The interview is being conducted in Chestnut Hill, in Brookline, Massachusetts, in the United States of America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your complete name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Benjamin Jacobs, J-A-C-O-B-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell your first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-E-N-J-A-M-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth is [? Dov, ?] [SPEAKING HEBREW], in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is Jacobs the original name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacobs is the second name, which I assumed when I became a citizen of the United States. My original name was Jakubowicz. It is my family name. My first name was Berek, B-E-R-E-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you spell Jakubowicz?

speaker: interviewee
text: J-A-K-O-B-O-- J-A-K-U-B-O-W-I-C-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at birth, your name was Berek--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jakubowicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Jakubowicz. And why did you change it to Jacobs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't my decision. I think being unexperienced as an American in the first weeks that I came, when I received the first paper, so-called, now green card, my uncle, great uncle, decided that I should Americanize my name into Jacobs. And so I did. I couldn't argue with my grand-- or great uncle, because he was my rescuer. He went from highest office to any agency to find out where I am alive with my brother and whoever else is alive of our family. And when he found us, he didn't rest till he brought us to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nicknames at all that you were called as a youngster?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not particularly. The nicknames wasn't a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very-- nicknames were not really a fashion in those days, when I was growing up in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: November 18, 1919.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your present age?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in a small town called Dobra-- D-O-B-R-A, that is in the region of Poznan. The next large city to us was Kalisz. And that is spelled K-A-L-I-C-Z-- S-Z, rather, S-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dobra was in what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Poland, in the region of Wartegau.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe Dobra, growing up as a youngster? To start off with, what was the population, the size, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dobra had approximately 150 Jewish families. The total number of population in Dobra was about 4,000. And of those, perhaps we were the largest minority, just about 10% or 11%, which was the average in Poland for Jews. To 35 million, there were 3 and 1/2 million Jews in Poland. But there also were living in Dobra other nationalities-- Russians, Germans predominantly, they were probably the second largest minority, and some Belorussians [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and Lithuanians. So that was a sort of a mix of four or five different nationalities and-- and races.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Dobra largely a commercial city? Or was it a agricultural-- how would you describe its industry?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, commercial city, it was-- a commercial city it was not. Dobra was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] rather unimportant agricultural city. I don't believe that Dobra has supplied internationally its products. But it has fed the local population. Farmers from nearby-- pardon me-- brought their wares to the city, brought their goods to the city-- to the little town, rather. And where trade was principally between the townspeople and the people that came with eggs and chickens and cows and ducks and-- and things to sell and wheat and-- and corn and things like that, they usually bought from the city coal and seeds and clothing and shoes and whatever the small-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people having small businesses in the city were producing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the home where you grew up in Dobra? Do you remember what street it was on?

speaker: interviewee
text: The street was [INAUDIBLE]. The number, it only seems to me remotely as being somewhat near 196 or 98. It was at the outskirts of the city. And generally, our neighbors were generally non-Jews, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with which we lived in a sense, up till a certain point, in a good committee and a good understanding and a neighborhood kind of an atmosphere, with tolerating one another and living with one another. Those are the memories of mine when I was a young man, up till about the age of 13 or 14, when I was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] exposed to the first anti-Jewish [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] first anti-Jewish feeling that was expressed to me by friends and by neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the house look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our house was a brick house, one-story house with five rooms. We had in back a barn and four smaller [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] barns, in which we had two cows and several horses, four acres of land, and a yard large enough to house some chicken and ducks and geese and turkeys, whatever we have growing ourself. Pardon me. We had also grown our own produce, such as potatoes and vegetables. And we also grew some wheat and corn and-- and oats for the horses, for the cows. And we worked in the land, each one of us-- my father, my mother, my sister, my brother, and myself, and my grandfather, who lived with us as long as I can remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the main language that was spoken in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say there were two languages that were spoken. The older folks-- that is my mother, father, and grandfather-- spoke Yiddish, while we, the young, spoke Polish among ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Awigdor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a merchant, trading in grains, in coal, in seeds and kind of produce that could be sold and bought from the farmers that came.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your father? What-- what was he like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a simple-- excuse me-- my father was a simple, devoted person to his family. He neither knew how to write or read. But his responsibilities were highly regarded, by each institution in the city. The banks and-- and lending institutions would lend him money, just on making three crosses instead of his name. And his word was, was as good as gold, as they say. And he was respected. He was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say a centralist, in terms of Jewish faith. He believed in God. We kept a kosher house. He took the children to the shul on Friday night and on Shabbos morning. And of course on every holiday, we received-- each holiday in-- during Yom Kippur, Rosh Hashana, and during Passover-- fresh clothes to go to shul. He had his own seat for his lifetime at the shul. He [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was always given mitzvot and-- in the shul. And he lived a life that one can say was completely devoted to his family, more so than to his work. His family was so precious to him that when he traveled somewhere, and he always brought something back. For example, oranges in those days were a luxury. And just the oranges that we have-- had in Poland were usually coming from Palestine, from Israel. And there were these so-called bloody oranges, with red inside. And if he would buy some oranges somewhere, which we couldn't buy in Dobra, he would bring them to us and share them with us, always. And he always would bring something for his children, whether it is a small gift-- usually small gifts, because we were not that affluent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other special or fond memory of something you did with him, as a youngster?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to love to drive with him, as he was visiting the German Junkers that had large [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] farms around our area. Well, he traded with them, and he was usually going to visit them. And he would take me into these so-called [POLISH] [POLISH], which means the very upscale houses and-- and seats of these Junkers and introduce me to them. I would spend some time with their children. And they would take me out in the gardens. And I would pick cherries, and we would pick apples, and we would pick pears. And I would usually get some to take home with me. This was fun times for me as I was growing up. And it was something that my father was proud of having me around when he went out. And he often took me with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Ester.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maiden name was also Jakubowicz, although they were not related to one another.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no relation at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No relation whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just the same name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not as far as I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have an occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a housewife. She did not have an occupation. In those days, usually women and occupation were two diverse things.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a kind, gentle, understanding, caring person not only for her family but also for people that were in need. I can remember that Fridays afternoons, she would pack a little food in a little basket and send me around to those that were poorer than we were, and whether it was challah or some cooked chicken or some cooked fish, which was always the norm to have for Friday night. However, there were some fruits in there and some gifts. I remember that was her standard Friday afternoon order to me. That was my mission. She also cared for the poor that came to our town, to gypsies, for example. They didn't need to steal from us. They knew they could come into Mother, and she would give them whatever she had and oftentimes more so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any special or fond memory that you remember? Anything that you did with her together, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: All my [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] young years with my family were special. Things that-- so far away but yet so close to me, whenever I do have a time to remember, whether it was with my mother, my father, my brother, or my sister. We were a family. Family was very, very, very special and very cherished and very caring and very understandable to one another. We didn't need to go outside for entertainment or have television in the house or even radios. We had ourself to traffic with, to talk with, to exchange ideas with, to just plainly talk and be around when it's being talked. And there were friends that used to come to our house, especially on Shabbat. Neighbors and friends, they would come in, and they would kvetch, and they would talk. And we as children would listen, and we would learn in process. My fondest memories that I have of all of my family is my grandfather who taught me a great deal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Hersh Yankev. That's two names, Hersh and Yankev.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your grand-- this was your father's--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was my maternal grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, only in glory. He was a wonderful man. He was tall for those days, over six feet. He was-- he wore a goatee and neatly trimmed. He was always clean and caring about himself. He had his own eyesight. He wore no glasses. He had his own teeth at the age of 67 or 68. In those days, those were old people. They usually had toothless mouths and wore glasses and looked haggardy. But my grandfather was sort of like a prosperous-looking individual. He wore a cane with a silver head and walked with a cane not for support but rather for status. He played chess with the townspeople, not necessarily Jewish. He played [NON-ENGLISH], which was a game that was played in Poland among the intelligentsia. He also played bridge in those days. And he taught me how to play chess, taught me how to play bridge. My younger days, he also took me fishing very often, which he liked. He was a-- an accomplished fisherman. He knew where the fish are, and when they could be caught and how to cast and what to use for bait. He wouldn't settle for anything except the best cast and this cleanest landing of the fish. He taught me how to be patient. He said, you have to be patient. Just like with people, that's how you have to handle your fish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I regret that I did not have more years with him. Because as the Germans occupied Dobra, he was once ridiculed in town, and his little goatee was set afire. He was humiliated, and he came into the house and he vowed to himself that he will never go out in the street again and lived a self-imposed, torturous few months. And he grew thinner and weaker and lost all his humor and his friends. And he missed the shul, and he missed the people. And he died one day in his sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: I sat next to him in the morning. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I suddenly discovered [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in his face a sort of a quizzing [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] look. How could this happen to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: He couldn't understand the world that has suddenly changed in such a way. And his [? sully ?] hair brown [? all of the ?] sudden, his hands were gnawed with arthritis. His skin was thin and darkened from the working in the fields and from the sun. And I had the feeling that he knew that I was with him in the hours of his death. But he didn't say it. I will always remember my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your other grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have never known my other grandparents. My grandfathers-- my grandmother on my maternal side died a few years before I was born. As a matter of fact, I have been given a name after her. Her name was Baily, and I was named Berek. My-- pardon me. My father's parents died long before I was born. As a matter of fact, he was just a child when he became a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when he lost his parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned you had a brother and a sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the oldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest was my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Josek. J-O-S-E-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe him as a youngster? What was he like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Josek was quite different than I am, than I was. Josek was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] less passionate, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] deliberate, patient, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] reserved [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] not a thinker, in a way. He was-- left the heavy questions outside his mind, while my sister and I usually spoke about affairs which concerned our future, affairs which concerned survival, affairs which concerned which is our way to go, whether it is Zionism or socialism. My sister was very much a socialist. She was-- she believed that our survival should be tied to the social agenda, which proclaimed that everyone is equal and that no religion should be different. She would have been very disappointed, would she have lived beyond 1955, when it became obvious that socialism was not for us a rescue. While I was a Zionist. I was a member of the Halutz Hashomer, or Halutz [NON-ENGLISH] Hatzair, which was a moderate, middle-of-the-road kind of an organization, which neither went to the left nor to the right. Affiliated itself less with religion and more with pioneering for Israel. And so my sister and I were two opposite poles of the spectrum. And we both debated very often, while my brother would have no interest in such matters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister's name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister's name was Pola, P-O-L-A, in Yiddish, Pearl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any memories of anything you might have done with your brother and sister as youngsters? Anything stand out in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, pardon me. Although I was quite younger-- I was five years younger than my brother and three years younger than my sister-- I always felt because I grew up among those older two brother and sister, I felt that I'm as grown up as they are. They treated me that way, and that's how I felt. But when it came to certain activities where at 19-year-old one could take part in, partake, and I could not as a 14-year-old one, I was very unhappy. And so I nudged and nudged till they took me along. And I was kind of introduced to a more mature life than I was. And I appreciate that very much. Very often my brother would not take me along, and my sister would. And that is whether they had gone to another town for a organizational meeting or went just as friends to visit friends. I was sort of like the fifth wheel, if you will. We also [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] participated in a dramatic circle, which was produced-- which the plays in Dobra. And oftentimes, we went out of Dobra with the play, and I was always part of it. Even so that I was 14 years old, I sometime played a 20-year-old young man. They dressed me up in a long suit, put me on a chair. And there I was, performing for, for I said a 20-year-old man.

speaker: interviewer
text: We had various Yiddish plays, mostly Yiddish plays. Do you remember the name of any?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of them that I remember is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The other one is [INAUDIBLE]. And there were two others, in which I had a very prominent role. I do not remember the name anymore. But it-- it went something like-- it was a rich Jewish assimilated family with a daughter. And I was the butler to the man of the house. And I was supposed to know when the man of the house uttered the first word what he wanted and what he meant to say. And so I was supposed to reply-- for example, as he would say, 'and,' then I would say, I will pick you up tonight, and I will have this and this ready, I will have this and this. And the play went down as the parents left and the daughter was alone, they-- she invited some friends, all university students, as she was, for a party. And they drank some spirits.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in comes a local, very poor Jew, who was regarded as a [NON-ENGLISH], as a dummy. He was made fun of. And then finally, they asked him-- after making lots of fun of him, and he kind of meek, walking around-- ask him, if you were to marry anyone of these girls, who would you like to marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he wouldn't respond. He says, leave me alone. I don't want to marry. I don't want anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one of these young university students lifted up his finger and pointed at the daughter of the house. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] faith behold, they put them on next to each other. And in fun, they married him, and he had to say [NON-ENGLISH], all those words that are in [NON-ENGLISH]. And when the father came back, he came just in as they were married. He says, what have you done? You are married to this man. And what in our faith, you must be his wife. Or else you must divorce. We-- we have to stop this tape. We'll continue with this story on tape number two shortly.
